metadata.subject: Mathematics
metadata.description: Mathematics 9 extends number sense to rational numbers and exponent laws, introduces polynomial operations, and develops algebraic reasoning through linear equations and two-variable relations. Students apply proportional and spatial reasoning through scale diagrams and similarity, and critically engage with data and statistics in social contexts. Financial literacy — including budgeting — connects mathematics to real-world decision making. First Peoples mathematical knowledge and perspectives are woven throughout.
metadata.general_description: An intermediate mathematics course extending rational number operations, exponent laws, polynomial algebra, linear relations, spatial reasoning with similarity, and statistical literacy — bridging toward senior mathematics pathways.

big_ideas.statement: Number represents and describes quantity; algebraic reasoning describes mathematical relationships.
big_ideas.elaboration: Operations with rational numbers — positive and negative fractions and decimals — extend earlier number sense and form the foundation for algebraic reasoning and equation solving.

big_ideas.statement: Computational fluency develops from a strong sense of number and extends to rational number operations.
big_ideas.elaboration: Fluency with rational number operations — including fractions, decimals, integers, and order of operations — builds the efficiency and flexibility necessary for algebraic reasoning.

big_ideas.statement: Patterns help represent regularities; continuous linear relationships can be described and represented.
big_ideas.elaboration: Linear patterns and relationships in tables, graphs, and equations describe proportional change. Recognizing, representing, and analyzing these patterns is foundational to algebra.

big_ideas.statement: Similar shapes possess proportional relationships that can be described and compared.
big_ideas.elaboration: Similarity extends the concepts of ratio and proportion to geometry — enabling measurement of indirect distances, construction of scale diagrams, and spatial reasoning.

big_ideas.statement: Analyzing data validity, reliability, and representation enables comparison and interpretation.
big_ideas.elaboration: Critical analysis of data — considering bias, sampling methods, and how data is displayed — is an essential civic skill for evaluating claims in media, politics, and everyday life.

competencies.raw: Apply logic and patterns to solve puzzles and games; employ reasoning to explore and apply mathematical ideas; estimate reasonably; apply mental math strategies; use technology to explore patterns and test ideas; model mathematics in contextualized experiences.
i_can_statements: I can use logical reasoning and pattern recognition to solve mathematical problems.
I can estimate reasonably with rational numbers and judge whether my answers make sense.
I can apply mental math strategies flexibly for rational number and algebraic computations.
I can use graphing technology to explore linear relations and test algebraic conjectures.
I can model real-world problems using rational numbers, ratios, and linear relationships.

competencies.raw: Apply multiple strategies to solve abstract and contextualized problems; develop understanding through play, inquiry, and problem solving; visualize to explore concepts; engage in problem-solving connected to place, story, and cultural practices.
i_can_statements: I can choose and apply multiple strategies to solve algebraic and geometric problems.
I can visualize and sketch diagrams to support understanding of linear relations and similarity.
I can solve problems connected to real-world, cultural, and place-based contexts.

competencies.raw: Use mathematical vocabulary in discussions; explain and justify mathematical ideas; communicate thinking concretely, pictorially, and symbolically; represent ideas in multiple forms.
i_can_statements: I can use correct mathematical vocabulary when discussing algebra, data, and geometry.
I can explain and justify my mathematical reasoning using multiple representations.
I can represent relationships using tables, graphs, equations, and diagrams.

competencies.raw: Reflect on mathematical thinking; connect concepts across disciplines and personal interests; use arguments to support personal choices; incorporate First Peoples worldviews and perspectives.
i_can_statements: I can reflect on my problem-solving approaches and identify what strategies were most effective.
I can connect math to science, social studies, and my community.
I can incorporate First Peoples mathematical knowledge and cultural contexts into my mathematical thinking.

areas.name: Number
areas.raw: Operations with rational numbers and exponents.
topics: Operations with rational numbers: addition, subtraction, multiplication, division (fractions and decimals)
Order of operations with rational numbers (BEDMAS)
Exponents: whole-number exponents and exponent laws (product, quotient, power of a power)
Square roots and irrational numbers: estimation on number line

areas.name: Algebra
topics: Operations with polynomials: collecting like terms, adding and subtracting polynomials (degree ≤ 2)
Multi-step one-variable linear equations: solving, verifying, and contextual problems
Two-variable linear relations: graphing, tables of values, and identifying slope

areas.name: Geometry and Spatial Reasoning
areas.raw: Spatial proportional reasoning, similarity, and scale diagrams.
topics: Similar polygons: identifying, comparing, and calculating missing side lengths
Scale diagrams: creating and interpreting with metric and imperial units
Spatial proportional reasoning: indirect measurement using similar triangles

areas.name: Statistics
areas.raw: Statistics in society: bias, sampling, reliability, and interpretation.
topics: Data collection: surveys, sampling methods (random, stratified, convenience)
Bias: identifying and evaluating sources of bias in data collection and display
Misleading graphs: distorted scales, incomplete data, and misrepresentation
Interpreting statistics in media and social contexts

areas.name: Financial Literacy
topics: Budgeting: income, fixed and variable expenses, and savings goals
Percent applications: discounts, taxes (GST/PST), gratuities, and simple interest
Comparing financial options: purchasing decisions using ratios and proportions
